IrradiationEffect_PKA_001.label: Primary Knock-On Atom (PKA)
IrradiationEffect_PKA_001.description: A lattice atom that receives sufficient kinetic energy from an incident particle to be displaced from its site, initiating the radiation damage event.

properties.creationMechanism: energy transfer from incident particle to lattice atom

source.text_excerpt: giving birth to a primary knock-on atom (PKA)

IrradiationEffect_DisplacementCascade_00.label: Displacement Cascade
IrradiationEffect_DisplacementCascade_00.description: A collection of point defects created by the PKA as it passes through the lattice and creates additional knock-on atoms.

source.text_excerpt: production of a displacement cascade

IrradiationEffect_DefectReactions_001.label: Defect Reactions
IrradiationEffect_DefectReactions_001.description: Thermal migration of point defects and defect clusters leading to recombination, clustering, trapping, and defect emission at timescales greater than 10^-8 seconds.

source.text_excerpt: Defect reactions by thermal migration

IrradiationEffect_ElasticScattering_001.label: Neutron Elastic Scattering
IrradiationEffect_ElasticScattering_001.description: Elastic collisions between neutrons and nuclei represented as colliding hard spheres, transferring recoil energy to the struck atom.

source.text_excerpt: elastic scattering between neutrons and nuclei

IrradiationEffect_InelasticScattering_00.label: Neutron Inelastic Scattering
IrradiationEffect_InelasticScattering_00.description: Reaction where emitted particle is experimentally the same as captured particle but with loss of kinetic energy found in excitation energy of product nucleus.

source.text_excerpt: Inelastic scattering is characterized

IrradiationEffect_N2nReaction_001.label: (n, 2n) Reaction
IrradiationEffect_N2nReaction_001.description: Nuclear reaction where a second neutron is emitted after residual excitation exceeds neutron binding energy, producing additional neutrons for damage or transmutation.

properties.model: based on Odette and Segev work

source.text_excerpt: Reactions such as the (n, 2n) reaction

IrradiationEffect_NgReaction_001.label: (n, γ) Reaction
IrradiationEffect_NgReaction_001.description: Radiative capture reaction where compound nucleus de-excites by emitting γ-ray, causing recoiling nucleus with sufficient energy to displace atoms.

source.text_excerpt: Another class of reactions involves photon

Defect_Vacancy_001.label: Vacancy
Defect_Vacancy_001.description: Point defect created when an atom is displaced from its lattice site, leaving an empty site in the crystal structure.

source.text_excerpt: vacancies and interstitials

Defect_Interstitial_001.label: Interstitial Atom (SIA)
Defect_Interstitial_001.description: Atom that has been knocked out of its lattice site and comes to rest in an interstitial position between regular lattice sites.

properties.termination: PKA comes to rest as interstitial

source.text_excerpt: single interstitial atoms (SIA)

Defect_FrenkelPair_001.label: Frenkel Pair
Defect_FrenkelPair_001.description: Pair of point defects consisting of a vacancy and an interstitial atom created during the radiation damage event.

source.text_excerpt: Stable Frenkel pairs

Defect_DefectCluster_001.label: Defect Cluster
Defect_DefectCluster_001.description: Aggregation of point defects (vacancies and interstitials) into larger defect structures formed during radiation damage.

properties.evolution: can grow or dissolve by thermal migration

source.text_excerpt: clusters of these defects

MaterialProperty_Gamma_nH_001.label: Gamma Parameter for n-H Interaction
MaterialProperty_Gamma_nH_001.description: Energy transfer parameter γ for neutron-hydrogen collision, representing maximum fraction of energy transferred from neutron to hydrogen atom.

source.text_excerpt: For a neutron incident on a hydrogen atom

MaterialProperty_Gamma_nU_001.label: Gamma Parameter for n-U Interaction
MaterialProperty_Gamma_nU_001.description: Energy transfer parameter γ for neutron-uranium collision, representing maximum fraction of energy transferred from neutron to uranium atom.

properties.significance: limited energy transfer due to large mass

source.text_excerpt: For a neutron incident on a uranium atom

MaterialProperty_Gamma_nC_001.label: Gamma Parameter for n-C Interaction
MaterialProperty_Gamma_nC_001.description: Energy transfer parameter γ for neutron-carbon collision, representing maximum fraction of energy transferred from neutron to carbon atom.

source.text_excerpt: 1MeV n on C: γ = 0.28, T̄ = 0.14MeV

MaterialProperty_Gamma_nFe_001.label: Gamma Parameter for n-Fe Interaction
MaterialProperty_Gamma_nFe_001.description: Energy transfer parameter γ for neutron-iron collision, representing maximum fraction of energy transferred from neutron to iron atom.

source.text_excerpt: 1MeV n on Fe: γ = 0.069, T̄ = 0.035MeV

MaterialProperty_Gamma_XeFe_001.label: Gamma Parameter for Xe-Fe Interaction
MaterialProperty_Gamma_XeFe_001.description: Energy transfer parameter γ for 100 keV Xe+ ion collision with iron atom.

properties.significance: high energy transfer from heavy ion

source.text_excerpt: value of γ for the Xe-Fe interaction is 0.83

MaterialProperty_Gamma_eFe_001.label: Gamma Parameter for e-Fe Interaction
MaterialProperty_Gamma_eFe_001.description: Energy transfer parameter γ for electron collision with iron atom, showing very limited energy transfer due to mass difference.

source.text_excerpt: value of γ for e-Fe interaction is 0.00004

MaterialProperty_ScatteringCrossSection_.label: Differential Scattering Cross Section

properties.definition: probability of collision with given energy and angle

MaterialProperty_EnergyTransferTime_001.label: Energy Transfer Time Scale

MaterialProperty_DisplacementTime_001.label: Displacement Time Scale

MaterialProperty_StableDefectTime_001.label: Stable Defect Formation Time

ch16_dislocation_loop_001.description: Dislocation loops (faulted or unfaulted, vacancy or interstitial type) that contribute to irradiation hardening

ch16_dislocation_line_001.description: Dislocation loops that have unfaulted and joined the dislocation network of the original microstructure

source.text_excerpt: Dislocation lines (dislocation loops that have unfaulted and joined the dislocation network of the original microstructure)

ch16_yield_strength_001.description: Yield strength (σy) - the stress at which a material begins to deform plastically, significantly increased by irradiation

ch16_ultimate_tensile_strength_001.label: Ultimate Tensile Strength
ch16_ultimate_tensile_strength_001.description: Maximum stress that a material can withstand while being stretched or pulled, increased less than yield strength under irradiation

source.text_excerpt: Irradiation also increases the yield strength, σy much more than it does the ultimate tensile strength, σUTS

ch16_ductility_001.label: Ductility
ch16_ductility_001.description: Ability of a material to deform under tensile stress, measured by total elongation or uniform elongation, reduced under irradiation

source.text_excerpt: the ductility (measured either by total elongation or by uniform elongation) is reduced

ch16_uniform_elongation_001.label: Uniform Elongation
ch16_uniform_elongation_001.description: Elongation during uniform plastic deformation before necking begins, approaches zero under irradiation when σy approaches σUTS

source.text_excerpt: The approach of σy to σUTS results in a loss of ductility to the limit where σy = σUTS and uniform elongation is zero

ch16_total_elongation_001.label: Total Elongation
ch16_total_elongation_001.description: Total strain at fracture, used as a measure of ductility, reduced under irradiation

source.text_excerpt: the ductility (measured either by total elongation or by uniform elongation) is reduced

ch16_necking_loss_001.label: Loss of Necking Deformation
ch16_necking_loss_001.description: Disappearance of necking deformation in bcc metals at low temperature with high fluence, leading to totally brittle fracture

condition.value: bcc metals, low temperature, high fluence

source.text_excerpt: In bcc metals tested at low temperature, high fluences may even result in the disappearance of necking deformation with fracture occurring on the elastic line

irradiation_hardening_ch17_p1.label: Irradiation Hardening
irradiation_hardening_ch17_p1.description: Increase in yield strength due to radiation-induced defects including defect clusters, impurity-defect complexes, dislocation loops, dislocation lines, voids, bubbles, and precipitates. Most pronounced at irradiation temperatures below 0.3Tm.

source.text_excerpt: Irradiation-induced hardening in both fcc and bcc metals is caused by the production of the various defects

ductility_reduction_ch17_p1.label: Irradiation-Induced Ductility Reduction
ductility_reduction_ch17_p1.description: Reduction in ductility (measured by total elongation or uniform elongation) due to irradiation. The approach of yield strength to ultimate tensile strength results in loss of ductility. At limit where yield strength equals ultimate tensile strength, uniform elongation is zero.

source.text_excerpt: the ductility (measured either by total elongation or by uniform elongation) is reduced

source_hardening_ch17_p1.label: Source Hardening
source_hardening_ch17_p1.description: Increase in stress required to start a dislocation moving on its glide plane due to irradiation-produced defect clusters in the vicinity of Frank-Read sources. The stress required to release a dislocation is called the unpinning or unlocking stress.

source.text_excerpt: Source hardening is the increase in stress required to start a dislocation moving on its glide plane

friction_hardening_ch17_p1.label: Friction Hardening
friction_hardening_ch17_p1.description: Stress required to sustain plastic deformation, often termed the flow stress or friction stress. Caused by long-range and short-range obstacles including dislocation network, defect clusters, loops, precipitates, and voids that impede dislocation motion.

source.text_excerpt: Friction hardening refers to the stress required to sustain plastic deformation

total_brittleness_ch17_p1.label: Irradiation-Induced Total Brittleness
total_brittleness_ch17_p1.description: In bcc metals tested at low temperature with high fluences, necking deformation may disappear and fracture occurs on the elastic line, rendering them totally brittle.

source.text_excerpt: high fluences may even result in the disappearance of necking deformation with fracture occurring on the elastic line

bcc_upper_lower_yield_ch17_p1.label: BCC Upper and Lower Yield Points
bcc_upper_lower_yield_ch17_p1.description: Manifestation of source hardening in bcc metals showing upper and lower yield points in stress-strain curves, caused by pinning or locking of dislocation lines by impurity atoms.

source.text_excerpt: In unirradiated bcc metals, source hardening is manifest by the upper and lower yield points

fcc_gradual_yielding_ch17_p1.label: FCC Gradual Yielding
fcc_gradual_yielding_ch17_p1.description: Gradual onset of yielding characteristic in fcc metals, explained by distribution of stresses required to operate Frank-Read sources. At low applied stress, dislocation sources with large separation between pinning points begin to generate dislocations.

source.text_excerpt: The gradual onset of yielding characteristic in fcc metals is generally explained by a distribution of stresses

loop_unlocking_ch17_p1.label: Loop Unlocking Hardening
loop_unlocking_ch17_p1.description: Hardening mechanism where dislocation line segments unlock from small dislocation loops or defect clusters at stresses lower than required to bow and operate a Frank-Read source. For edge character loops with Burgers vector bl, radius r, and spacing l, the shear stress is approximately 0.09μb/l.

source.text_excerpt: Singh suggests that y = 1.5r is consistent with the observed microstructure

dipole_network_hardening_ch17_p1.label: Dislocation Dipole Network Hardening
dipole_network_hardening_ch17_p1.description: Yield stress estimated by stress necessary to overcome interaction between dislocation dipoles when loops form a network. Shear stress is approximately 0.06μb/y where y is the distance between dipoles.

source.text_excerpt: unlocking process occurred by interaction between an edge segment and a network of loops

long_range_stress_hardening_ch17_p1.label: Long-Range Stress Hardening
long_range_stress_hardening_ch17_p1.description: Forces arising from repulsive interaction between a moving dislocation and components of the dislocation network. Stress depends on dislocation density ρd as σLR = αμb√ρd where α ≈ 0.44.

source.text_excerpt: Long-range forces arise from the repulsive interaction between a moving dislocation

short_range_stress_hardening_ch17_p1.label: Short-Range Stress Hardening
short_range_stress_hardening_ch17_p1.description: Forces due to interaction between moving dislocation and discrete obstacles in the slip plane. Includes athermal (dislocation bowing) and thermally activated (cutting or climbing) processes. Total friction stress σF = σLR + σSR where σSR = σppt + σvoid + σloops.

source.text_excerpt: Short-range forces are due to the interaction between a moving dislocation and an obstacle

hall_petch_hardening_ch17_p1.label: Hall-Petch Grain Size Hardening
hall_petch_hardening_ch17_p1.description: Yield strength dependence on grain size given by σy = σi + kyd^(-1/2) where σi is friction stress, ky is Hall-Petch coefficient, and d is grain diameter. Yield strength increases with decreasing grain size for nominal grain sizes (few to hundreds of micrometers).

trend.value: increasing strength with decreasing grain size

source.text_excerpt: Equation (12.63) is the Hall–Petch equation, which describes the grain size dependence of the yield stress

yield_strength_ch17_p1.label: Yield Strength
yield_strength_ch17_p1.description: Stress at which plasticity begins, a key parameter in determining mechanical behavior of metals. In irradiated materials, yield strength increases much more than ultimate tensile strength, leading to ductility reduction.

source.text_excerpt: The stress at which plasticity begins is called the yield stress, σy or YS

ultimate_tensile_strength_ch17_p1.label: Ultimate Tensile Strength
ultimate_tensile_strength_ch17_p1.description: Maximum load divided by original cross-sectional area of the sample. Irradiation increases yield strength much more than ultimate tensile strength, causing the approach of σy to σUTS and resulting in loss of ductility.

source.text_excerpt: The tensile strength or ultimate tensile strength (UTS) is the maximum load divided by the original cross sectional area

uniform_strain_ch17_p1.label: Uniform Strain
uniform_strain_ch17_p1.description: Strain that occurs uniformly in the gage section of the sample up to the ultimate tensile strength when necking begins. True uniform strain εu is equal to strain hardening exponent n (εu = n) in power-law hardening.

source.text_excerpt: Strain occurs uniformly in the gage section of the sample up to the UTS

true_uniform_strain_ch17_p1.label: True Uniform Strain
true_uniform_strain_ch17_p1.description: Natural logarithm of the ratio of original area to area at maximum load: εu = ln(A0/Au). In power-law hardening, true uniform strain equals the strain hardening exponent n.

source.text_excerpt: The true uniform strain, εu is given by the strain at maximum load

fracture_strain_ch17_p1.label: Fracture Strain
fracture_strain_ch17_p1.description: True strain based on original area and area after fracture: εf = ln(A0/Af) = ln(1/(1-RA)) where RA is reduction in area at fracture.

source.text_excerpt: The true fracture strain, εf is the true strain based on the original area and the area after fracture

local_necking_strain_ch17_p1.label: Local Necking Strain
local_necking_strain_ch17_p1.description: Strain required to deform the specimen from maximum load to fracture: εn = ln(Au/Af). Necking or localized deformation begins at the point of maximum load defined by dP = 0.

source.text_excerpt: the local necking strain, εn is the strain required to deform the specimen from the maximum load to fracture

strain_hardening_exponent_ch17_p1.label: Strain Hardening Exponent
strain_hardening_exponent_ch17_p1.description: Exponent n in the power-law hardening relationship σ = Kε^n, where σ is stress, εp is plastic strain, and K is stress at εp = 1.0. Parameter n is between 0.1 and 0.5 typically, with n = 0 for perfectly plastic and n = 1 for elastic behavior.

source.text_excerpt: n is the strain hardening exponent. Note that n = 0 for perfectly plastic behavior and n = 1 for elastic behavior

shear_modulus_ch17_p1.label: Shear Modulus
shear_modulus_ch17_p1.description: Material property relating shear stress to shear strain in elastic deformation. Appears in expressions for long-range stress hardening (σLR = αμb√ρd) and source hardening (σFR = μb/l).

source.text_excerpt: where μ is the shear modulus

poissons_ratio_ch17_p1.label: Poisson's Ratio
poissons_ratio_ch17_p1.description: Ratio of transverse strain to longitudinal strain in elastic deformation. Value is 0.25 for perfectly isotropic elastic materials, approximately 0.33 for most metals. Used in stress-strain relations and long-range stress calculations.

source.text_excerpt: where ν is Poisson's ratio and has a value of 0.25 for perfectly isotropic elastic materials

bulk_modulus_ch17_p1.label: Bulk Modulus
bulk_modulus_ch17_p1.description: Ratio of hydrostatic pressure to dilatation, K = E/(3(1-2ν)). Represents volumetric modulus of elasticity. The hydrostatic component of stress produces only elastic volume changes and does not cause plastic deformation.

role.value: ratio of hydrostatic pressure to dilatation

source.text_excerpt: K = E/(3(1-2ν)) is the bulk modulus or volumetric modulus of elasticity

youngs_modulus_ch17_p1.label: Young's Modulus
youngs_modulus_ch17_p1.description: Modulus of elasticity in tension or compression, E. In elastic region, deformation is proportional to load according to Hooke's law: σ = Eε where σ is stress and ε is strain.

source.text_excerpt: σ = Eε , where σ is stress, ε is strain and E is the modulus of elasticity in tension or compression

dislocation_density_ch17_p1.label: Dislocation Density
dislocation_density_ch17_p1.description: Number density of dislocations in material, ρd, affects long-range stress hardening. The distance between dislocations r is given by r = 1/√(πρd). Long-range stress σLR = αμb√ρd where α ≈ 0.44.

source.text_excerpt: where ρd is the dislocation density gives: FLR = μb²√πρd/π^(4/3)

von_mises_yield_criterion_ch17_p1.label: von Mises Yield Criterion
von_mises_yield_criterion_ch17_p1.description: Yielding occurs when second invariant of stress deviator exceeds critical value: σy = (1/√2)[(σ1-σ2)² + (σ2-σ3)² + (σ3-σ1)²]^(1/2). For pure shear, yield stress is k = σy/√3 = 0.577σy.

source.text_excerpt: von Mises proposed that yielding will occur when the second invariant of the stress deviator

tresca_yield_criterion_ch17_p1.label: Tresca Yield Criterion
tresca_yield_criterion_ch17_p1.description: Maximum shear stress criterion: yielding occurs when maximum shear stress reaches shear stress in uniaxial tension test. For pure shear, yield stress is k = σy/2. Observed to hold fairly well in alloys exhibiting a yield drop.

source.text_excerpt: Tresca (or maximum shear stress) criterion, which says that yielding occurs when the maximum shear stress

offset_yield_strength_ch17_p1.label: Offset Yield Strength
offset_yield_strength_ch17_p1.description: Commonly accepted definition of yield stress determined by stress corresponding to intersection of stress-strain curve and line parallel to elastic portion offset by strain of 0.2%.

source.text_excerpt: the offset yield strength, determined by the stress corresponding to the intersection of the stress-strain curve
